name: utah
description: Universally Triggered Agent Harness - An OpenClaw-like Inngest-powered personal agent

Add improved logger (#18)

commit.message:
Asynchronous sub-agents (#17)

* feat: add async sub-agent delegation

- Add delegate_async_task tool for long-running, fire-and-forget tasks
- Extend agent.subagent.spawn event with channel routing (channel, destination, channelMeta)
- Add channelRouting to AgentLoopOptions for passing context through
- Sub-agent detects async mode and replies directly to user via agent.reply.ready event
- Sync delegate_task unchanged - both modes available
- Failure handler already handles async failures via event routing

* docs: update SUB-AGENTS.md to reflect dual sync/async implementation

* feat: add delegate_scheduled_task tool for time-delayed sub-agent execution

- New tool schema requiring ISO 8601 timestamp for scheduling
- Uses Inngest step.sendEvent() with ts field for delayed delivery
- Sub-agent receives scheduling context for better user replies
- Same async sub-agent function, no new infrastructure needed

* feat: incremental replies during agent loop

When AGENT_INCREMENTAL_REPLIES=true, the agent emits agent.reply.ready
events for text returned alongside tool calls, giving users progress
updates during long-running loops. Emitted text is tracked to avoid
duplication in the final response.

Also captures event IDs from async/scheduled sub-agent spawns.

commit.message:
Sub-agents (#8)

* fix: push full AssistantMessage into messages array

The root cause: when the LLM returned tool calls, we pushed a partial
{role, content} object into the messages array. On the next iteration,
pi-ai's transformMessages() casts all assistant messages to AssistantMessage
and calls content.flatMap() — but content was a raw array, not the
structured content blocks array with the required AssistantMessage metadata.

Changes:
- callLLM() now returns the full AssistantMessage from complete() as 'message'
- Agent loop pushes llmResponse.message directly (full pi-ai AssistantMessage)
- History entries construct proper AssistantMessage objects with content blocks
- All user/toolResult messages include required 'timestamp' field
- LLM errors now throw (letting Inngest retry) instead of returning silently
- validateToolArguments call includes required 'type: toolCall' field

* fix: resolve all TypeScript errors

- llm.ts: use KnownProvider type assertion for getModel() runtime config
- tools.ts: convert remember tool from JSON Schema to TypeBox (Type.Object)
- transform.ts: narrow msg.text with non-null assertion after guard
- heartbeat.ts: add required timestamp to user message
- compaction.ts: import SessionMessage from session.ts, remove duplicate
  interface, simplify to match string-only session content
- context.ts: return SessionMessage[] from buildConversationHistory
  instead of stripping timestamp
- session.ts: export SessionMessage interface
- agent-loop.ts: annotate history type, remove sendEvent from StepAPI
  (not used by loop), simplify history map type

* feat: replace custom tools with pi-coding-agent implementations

Swap Utah's basic file tools for pi-coding-agent's battle-tested coding tools:

- read: offset/limit support, image handling, binary detection, smart truncation
- edit: exact text match + replace (surgical edits, not full file rewrites)
- write: file creation with directory creation
- bash: shell execution with configurable timeout and output truncation
- grep: regex search respecting .gitignore
- find: glob-based file discovery respecting .gitignore
- ls: directory listing with tree display

Custom tools (remember, web_fetch) kept as Utah-specific additions.

Also updates system prompt with tool-aware guidelines matching
pi-coding-agent's recommended patterns (read before edit, prefer
grep/find/ls over bash for exploration, etc.).

* fix: prevent context overflow with memory cap + overflow recovery

Two changes:

1. Memory context capped at 10K chars (~2,500 tokens). Priority order
   preserves today's log > yesterday > long-term memory. Truncates
   from the top (oldest content) when over budget.

2. Context overflow detection in agent loop. When LLM returns overflow
   error, force-compacts messages (summarizes old, keeps last 6) and
   retries the iteration. Only attempts once per run to avoid loops.

* feat: add sub-agent spawning via delegate_task tool

Implements sub-agent system following docs/SUB-AGENTS.md design:

- New Inngest function 'agent-sub-agent' that runs an isolated agent loop
  with its own session key and fresh context window
- New 'delegate_task' tool that the main agent can use to offload complex
  work to a sub-agent via step.invoke()
- Sub-agent uses SUB_AGENT_TOOLS (no delegate_task) to prevent recursion
- Sub-agent session keys are scoped as sub-{parentSessionKey}-{timestamp}
- Parent only receives the sub-agent's final summary response, keeping
  its own context lean (94% token reduction for heavy tool work)
- createAgentLoop now accepts options for tool list and isSubAgent flag

Files changed:
- src/functions/sub-agent.ts (new) — sub-agent Inngest function
- src/lib/tools.ts — added delegate_task tool + SUB_AGENT_TOOLS export
- src/agent-loop.ts — options param, delegate_task handling via step.invoke
- src/client.ts — agent.subagent.spawn event type
- src/worker.ts — registered sub-agent function

---------

Co-authored-by: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

commit.message:
Set up support for multiple channels (#4)

* feat: add setup script for Inngest + Telegram webhook configuration

Runs at worker startup and standalone (node src/setup.ts):
1. Lists Inngest webhooks, finds or creates one by name
2. Compares transform source — updates if out of date
3. Sets Telegram bot webhook URL to point at Inngest webhook
4. Prints confirmation with bot info, webhook status, errors

Transform source of truth lives in setup.ts — single place to
update the transform, automatically synced on next startup.

* refactor: organize channel code into src/channels/telegram/

New structure:
  src/channels/
    setup-helpers.ts       — shared Inngest REST API client
    telegram/
      index.ts             — barrel exports
      api.ts               — Telegram Bot API client (shared)
      format.ts            — markdown→HTML, splitMessage, stripMarkdown
      transform.ts         — Inngest webhook transform source of truth
      setup.ts             — webhook setup (Inngest + Telegram)
      reply.ts             — Inngest function: send messages
      typing.ts            — Inngest function: typing indicator

Removed:
  src/functions/telegram-reply.ts
  src/functions/telegram-typing.ts
  src/transforms/telegram.ts
  Telegram code from src/setup.ts

Adding a new channel (e.g. Slack) means creating src/channels/slack/
with the same pattern and registering it in setup.ts + worker.ts.

* refactor: generic channel dispatch for reply + typing

Single Inngest functions that dispatch to any channel:
- src/functions/send-reply.ts — routes agent.reply.ready to channel handler
- src/functions/typing-indicator.ts — routes typing to channel handler

Channel abstraction:
- src/channels/types.ts — ChannelHandler interface (sendReply, sendTyping, setup)
- src/channels/index.ts — channel registry + getChannel() dispatcher
- src/channels/telegram/handler.ts — implements ChannelHandler for Telegram

Inngest function list is now fixed at 5 regardless of channel count:
  handle-message, send-reply, typing-indicator, failure-handler, heartbeat

Adding a channel = implement ChannelHandler + register in channels/index.ts

* refactor: rename sendTyping → acknowledge across channel abstraction

- ChannelHandler.sendTyping → ChannelHandler.acknowledge
- SendTypingParams → AcknowledgeParams (now includes messageId for reactions)
- typing-indicator function → acknowledge-message
- Telegram handler: acknowledge() sends typing indicator
- Future: Slack acknowledge() can add 👀 reaction to messageId

* feat: normalized channel events with destination + sender + channelMeta (#5)

* feat: normalized channel events with destination + sender + channelMeta

New event contract:
- sender: { id, name, username } — normalized across all channels
- destination: { chatId, messageId, threadId } — where to reply
- channelMeta: Record<string, unknown> — opaque channel-specific data

Flow: webhook → transform (produces normalized event) → agent loop
(reads message, sessionKey, sender) → reply event (passes through
channel, destination, channelMeta) → channel handler (reads all)

Changes:
- types.ts: AgentMessageData, AgentReplyData, Sender, Destination
- message.ts: concurrency key on destination.chatId, passes through meta
- send-reply.ts: dispatches with destination + channelMeta
- acknowledge-message.ts: dispatches with destination + channelMeta
- telegram/handler.ts: reads from destination instead of flat params
- telegram/transform.ts: produces new normalized shape
- docs/channel-event-normalization.md: full spec with 4 channel examples

* feat: first-class Inngest event schemas — remove all 'as' casts

Define event types on the Inngest client using EventSchemas.fromRecord<>():
- agent.message.received → AgentMessageData
- agent.reply.ready → AgentReplyData
- telegram/message.unsupported, telegram/transform.failed

All functions now get fully typed event.data without casting.
step.sendEvent() is also typed — emitting an agent.reply.ready with
wrong fields is a compile error.

Inngest function list is now fixed at 5 regardless of channel count:
  handle-message, send-reply, typing-indicator, failure-handler, heartbeat

Adding a channel = implement ChannelHandler + register in channels/index.ts

* refactor: rename sendTyping → acknowledge across channel abstraction

- ChannelHandler.sendTyping → ChannelHandler.acknowledge
- SendTypingParams → AcknowledgeParams (now includes messageId for reactions)
- typing-indicator function → acknowledge-message
- Telegram handler: acknowledge() sends typing indicator
- Future: Slack acknowledge() can add 👀 reaction to messageId

* feat: normalized channel events with destination + sender + channelMeta (#5)

* feat: normalized channel events with destination + sender + channelMeta

New event contract:
- sender: { id, name, username } — normalized across all channels
- destination: { chatId, messageId, threadId } — where to reply
- channelMeta: Record<string, unknown> — opaque channel-specific data

Flow: webhook → transform (produces normalized event) → agent loop
(reads message, sessionKey, sender) → reply event (passes through
channel, destination, channelMeta) → channel handler (reads all)

Changes:
- types.ts: AgentMessageData, AgentReplyData, Sender, Destination
- message.ts: concurrency key on destination.chatId, passes through meta
- send-reply.ts: dispatches with destination + channelMeta
- acknowledge-message.ts: dispatches with destination + channelMeta
- telegram/handler.ts: reads from destination instead of flat params
- telegram/transform.ts: produces new normalized shape
- docs/channel-event-normalization.md: full spec with 4 channel examples

* feat: first-class Inngest event schemas — remove all 'as' casts

Define event types on the Inngest client using EventSchemas.fromRecord<>():
- agent.message.received → AgentMessageData
- agent.reply.ready → AgentReplyData
- telegram/message.unsupported, telegram/transform.failed

All functions now get fully typed event.data without casting.
step.sendEvent() is also typed — emitting an agent.reply.ready with
wrong fields is a compile error.

commit.message:
Heartbeat memory (#3)

* feat: add heartbeat function for periodic memory maintenance

Cron-triggered Inngest function that:
1. Loads recent daily logs (last 7 days) + current MEMORY.md
2. Uses LLM to distill daily notes into curated long-term memory
3. Writes updated MEMORY.md
4. Prunes daily logs older than 14 days

Each step is durable — retries on failure, observable in Inngest dashboard.
Schedule configurable via HEARTBEAT_CRON env var (default: 4am daily).

* feat: adaptive heartbeat — only distills when needed

- Runs every 30min but most runs are just a file size check (no LLM)
- Triggers distillation when: daily log > 4KB OR > 8 hours since last
- Appends <!-- last_heartbeat: ISO --> to MEMORY.md for cheap parsing
- Strips timestamp before injecting memory into system prompt
- Light days: 1-2 distills. Heavy days: distills when logs get chunky.
- Configurable: HEARTBEAT_CRON, LOG_SIZE_THRESHOLD, MAX_HOURS_BETWEEN

* feat: configurable log retention via MEMORY_RETENTION_DAYS (default 30)

* refactor: centralize timestamp helpers in memory.ts

commit.message:
feat: add compaction + workspace context injection

Port from pi-inngest prototype:
- Context injection: reads IDENTITY.md, SOUL.md, USER.md, MEMORY.md
  from workspace dir and injects into system prompt
- Compaction: LLM-powered conversation summarization when context
  gets too long (80% of 150K token estimate). Runs as Inngest step.
- Placeholder workspace files for agent identity/personality
- Updated README with workspace context and compaction docs
- Updated project structure diagram

feat: swap LLM layer to pi-ai

Replace raw Anthropic API calls with pi-ai's complete() + getModel().
- TypeBox schemas for tool definitions (runtime validation)
- Multi-provider support (anthropic, openai, google) via LLM_PROVIDER env
- pi-ai message format (toolCall/toolResult vs tool_use/tool_result)
- Same Inngest architecture: singleton, cancelOn, step-based loop
- Same tools, context pruning, session persistence, Telegram integration
feat: default model to Claude Opus 4.6

Initial commit — durable AI agent example with Inngest

Think/act/observe loop with Inngest steps for durability.
Telegram integration via webhook transforms + event-driven replies.
Local development via connect(), singleton concurrency, cancel-on-new-message.
Context pruning, iteration budgets, global failure handler.

readme_html:
Inngest Agent Example — Utah

Universally Triggered Agent Harness

A durable AI agent built with Inngest and pi-ai. No framework. Just a think/act/observe loop — Inngest provides durability, retries, and observability, while pi-ai provides a unified LLM interface across providers.

Simple TypeScript that gives you:

  • 🔄 Durable agent loop — every LLM call and tool execution is an Inngest step
  • 🔁 Automatic retries — LLM API timeouts are handled by Inngest, not your code
  • 🔒 Singleton concurrency — one conversation at a time per chat, no race conditions
  • ⚡ Cancel on new message — user sends again? Current run cancels, new one starts
  • 📡 Multi-channel — Slack, Telegram, and more via a simple channel interface
  • 🏠 Local development — runs on your machine via connect(), no server needed

Architecture

Channel (e.g. Telegram) → Inngest Cloud (webhook + transform) → WebSocket → Local Worker → LLM (Anthropic/OpenAI/Google) → Reply Event → Channel API

The worker connects to Inngest Cloud via WebSocket. No public endpoint. No ngrok. No VPS. Messages flow through Inngest as events, and the agent processes them locally with full filesystem access.

Prerequisites

  • Node.js 23+ (uses native TypeScript strip-types)
  • LLM API key (e.g. Anthropic API key (console.anthropic.com))
  • Inngest account (app.inngest.com)
  • At least one channel configured (see Channels below)

Setup

1. Create an Inngest Account

  1. Sign up at app.inngest.com
  2. Go to Settings → Keys and copy your:
    • Event Key (for sending events)
    • Signing Key (for authenticating your worker)

2. Configure and Run

git clone https://github.com/inngest/utah
cd utah
npm install # or pnpm
cp .env.example .env

Edit .env with your keys:

ANTHROPIC_API_KEY=sk-ant-...
INNGEST_EVENT_KEY=...
INNGEST_SIGNING_KEY=signkey-prod-...

Then add the environment variables for your channel(s) — see setup guides below.

Start the worker:

# Production mode (connects to Inngest Cloud via WebSocket)
npm start

# Development mode (uses local Inngest dev server)
npx inngest-cli@latest dev &
npm run dev

On startup, the worker automatically sets up webhooks and transforms for each configured channel.

Channels

The agent supports multiple messaging channels. Each channel has its own setup guide:

  • Telegram — Fully automated setup. Just add your bot token and run.
  • Slack — Requires creating a Slack app and configuring Event Subscriptions.

Project Structure

src/
├── worker.ts                  # Entry point — connect() or serve()
├── client.ts                  # Inngest client
├── config.ts                  # Configuration from env vars
├── agent-loop.ts              # Core think → act → observe cycle
├── setup.ts                   # Channel setup orchestration
├── lib/
│   ├── llm.ts                 # pi-ai wrapper (multi-provider: Anthropic, OpenAI, Google)
│   ├── tools.ts               # Tool definitions (TypeBox schemas) + execution
│   ├── context.ts             # System prompt builder with workspace file injection
│   ├── session.ts             # JSONL session persistence
│   ├── memory.ts              # File-based memory system (daily logs + distillation)
│   └── compaction.ts          # LLM-powered conversation summarization
├── functions/
│   ├── message.ts             # Main agent function (singleton + cancelOn)
│   ├── send-reply.ts          # Channel-agnostic reply dispatch
│   ├── acknowledge-message.ts # Message acknowledgment (typing indicator, etc.)
│   ├── heartbeat.ts           # Cron-based memory maintenance
│   └── failure-handler.ts     # Global error handler with notifications
└── channels/
    ├── types.ts               # ChannelHandler interface
    ├── index.ts               # Channel registry
    ├── setup-helpers.ts       # Inngest REST API helpers for webhook setup
    └── <channel-name>/        # A channel implementation (see README for setup)
        ├── handler.ts         # ChannelHandler implementation
        ├── api.ts             # API client
        ├── setup.ts           # Webhook setup automation
        ├── transform.ts       # Webhook transform
        └── format.ts          # Formatting for channel messages
workspace/                       # Agent workspace (persisted across runs)
├── SOUL.md                    # Agent personality and behavioral guidelines
├── USER.md                    # User information
├── MEMORY.md                  # Long-term memory (agent-writable)
├── memory/                    # Daily logs (YYYY-MM-DD.md, auto-managed)
└── sessions/                  # JSONL conversation files (gitignored)

How It Works

The Agent Loop

The core is a while loop where each iteration is an Inngest step:

  1. Think — step.run("think") calls the LLM via pi-ai's complete()
  2. Act — if the LLM wants tools, each tool runs as step.run("tool-read")
  3. Observe — tool results are fed back into the conversation
  4. Repeat — until the LLM responds with text (no tools) or max iterations

Inngest auto-indexes duplicate step IDs in loops (think:0, think:1, etc.), so you don't need to track iteration numbers in step names.

Event-Driven Composition

One incoming message triggers multiple independent functions:

Function Purpose Config
agent-handle-message Run the agent loop Singleton per chat, cancel on new message
acknowledge-message Show "typing..." immediately No retries (best effort)
send-reply Format and send the response 3 retries, channel dispatch
agent-heartbeat Distill daily logs into long-term memory Cron (every 30 min)
global-failure-handler Catch errors, notify user Triggered by inngest/function.failed

Workspace Context Injection

The agent reads markdown files from the workspace directory and injects them into the system prompt:

File Purpose
SOUL.md Agent personality, behavioral guidelines, tone, boundaries
USER.md Info about the user (name, timezone, preferences)
MEMORY.md Curated long-term memory (agent-writable)

Edit these files to customize your agent's personality and knowledge. The agent can also update MEMORY.md using the write tool to remember things across conversations.

Memory System

The agent has a two-tier memory system:

  • Daily logs (workspace/memory/YYYY-MM-DD.md) — append-only notes written via the remember tool during conversations
  • Long-term memory (workspace/MEMORY.md) — curated summary distilled from daily logs by the heartbeat function

The agent-heartbeat function runs on a cron schedule (default: every 30 minutes). It checks if daily logs have accumulated enough content, then uses the LLM to distill them into MEMORY.md. Old daily logs are pruned after a configurable retention period (default: 30 days).

Conversation Compaction

Long conversations get summarized automatically so the agent doesn't lose context or hit token limits:

  • Token estimation: Uses a chars/4 heuristic to estimate conversation size
  • Threshold: Compaction triggers when estimated tokens exceed 80% of the configured max (150K)
  • LLM summarization: Old messages are summarized into a structured checkpoint (goals, progress, decisions, next steps)
  • Recent messages preserved: The most recent ~20K tokens of conversation are kept verbatim
  • Persisted: The compacted session replaces the JSONL file, so it survives restarts

Compaction runs as an Inngest step (step.run("compact")), so it's durable and retryable.

Context Pruning

Long tool results bloat the conversation context and cause the LLM to lose focus. The agent uses two-tier pruning:

  • Soft trim: Tool results over 4K chars get head+tail trimmed (first 1,500 + last 1,500 chars)
  • Hard clear: When total old tool content exceeds 50K chars, old results are replaced entirely
  • Budget warnings: System messages are injected when iterations are running low

Adding New Channels

The agent is channel-agnostic. Each channel implements a ChannelHandler interface (src/channels/types.ts) with methods for sending replies, acknowledging messages, and setup. Each channel directory follows the same structure:

src/channels/<name>/
├── handler.ts      # ChannelHandler implementation (sendReply, acknowledge)
├── api.ts          # API client for the channel's platform
├── setup.ts        # Webhook setup automation
├── transform.ts    # Plain JS transform for Inngest webhook
└── format.ts       # Markdown → channel-specific format conversion

To add Discord, WhatsApp, or any other channel:

  1. Create a new directory under src/channels/ following the structure above
  2. Implement the ChannelHandler interface in handler.ts
  3. Write a webhook transform that converts the channel's payload to agent.message.received
  4. Register the channel in src/channels/index.ts

The agent loop, reply dispatch, and acknowledgment functions are all channel-agnostic — no changes needed outside src/channels/.

Key Inngest Features Used

  • connect() — WebSocket-based worker
  • Singleton execution — one run per chat at a time
  • Step retries — automatic retry on LLM API failures
  • Event-driven functions — compose behavior from small focused functions
  • Webhook transforms — convert external payloads to typed events
  • Checkpointing — near-zero inter-step latency

Acknowledgments

This project uses pi-ai (@mariozechner/pi-ai) by Mario Zechner for its unified LLM interface and @mariozechner/pi-coding-agent for it's. standard tools. pi-ai provides a single complete() function that works across Anthropic, OpenAI, Google, and other providers — making it easy to swap models without changing any agent code. It's a great library.

License

Apache-2.0
